questions.question:
Which of the following is/are advantage(s) of virtual memory?
A. Faster access to memory on an average.
B. Processes can be given protected address spaces.
C. Linker can assign addresses independent of where the program will be loaded in physical memory.
D. Program larger than the physical memory size can be run.
questions.to_find: Which options are true advantages of virtual memory

understand.plain: Virtual memory is a memory management technique that gives each process the illusion of a large, private address space. We need to identify which of the listed properties are genuine advantages of virtual memory. Note that virtual memory does NOT make memory access faster on average because page faults add significant overhead.

keywords.explain: A technique that allows processes to use more memory than physically available by storing parts of the address space on disk and bringing them in on demand.
keywords.example: A process with 1 GB virtual space can run on a machine with only 512 MB RAM.

keywords.explain: An event that occurs when a process accesses a page not currently in physical memory, requiring a disk fetch.
keywords.example: Accessing an unmapped page triggers OS to load it from swap.

keywords.explain: Each process gets its own isolated virtual address space, preventing one process from corrupting another's memory.
keywords.example: Process A at virtual address 0x1000 and Process B at virtual address 0x1000 map to different physical frames.

understand.visual_alt: Virtual memory maps each process's large virtual address space to a smaller physical RAM and disk swap area.

given.aim: Identify which listed properties are genuine advantages of virtual memory.

terms.meaning: Claims virtual memory speeds up average memory access.
terms.example: Page faults actually SLOW down access.
terms.connects: This is FALSE — page faults cause disk I/O which is much slower than RAM.

terms.meaning: Each process gets its own isolated virtual address space.
terms.example: Processes cannot directly access each other's memory.
terms.connects: TRUE advantage — isolation and protection are key benefits of virtual memory.

terms.meaning: Linker assigns virtual addresses; actual physical placement is handled by OS at runtime.
terms.example: Two programs can both be linked at address 0x0000 and run simultaneously.
terms.connects: TRUE advantage — simplifies linking and loading.

terms.term: Option D: Programs larger than physical memory
terms.meaning: Virtual memory allows running programs whose total size exceeds physical RAM.
terms.example: A 2 GB program can run on a 512 MB machine using demand paging.
terms.connects: TRUE advantage — this is the primary motivation for virtual memory.

given.plan: Evaluate each option against the known properties of virtual memory. Option A is false because page faults cause overhead. Options B, C, and D are all valid advantages.

steps.title: Evaluate Option A: Faster access on average
steps.apply: When a page fault occurs (probability p), the system must access disk which takes milliseconds vs microseconds for RAM. So virtual memory INCREASES average access time, not decreases it. Option A is FALSE.
steps.note: Page faults are extremely costly; even a very low page fault rate can increase effective access time significantly.

steps.title: Evaluate Options B, C, D
steps.apply:
B: Each process gets its own page table mapping virtual to physical addresses, providing isolation and protection. TRUE.
C: Linker produces virtual addresses; the OS/MMU handles physical placement transparently. TRUE.
D: Demand paging allows only the needed pages to reside in RAM; the rest stays on disk. A program can be much larger than physical memory. TRUE.
steps.note: All three (B, C, D) are standard textbook advantages of virtual memory.

solution.result: B, C, and D are advantages of virtual memory. A is NOT an advantage (virtual memory does not make memory access faster on average).

questions.formula_note: Effective Memory Access Time formula: EAT = (1-p)*t_mem + p*t_pagefault, where p is page fault rate. Since t_pagefault >> t_mem, virtual memory increases average access time.

questions.question:
Suppose the time to service a page fault is on the average 10 milliseconds, while a memory access takes 1 microsecond. Then a 99.99% hit ratio results in average memory access time of
A. 1.9999 milliseconds
B. 1 millisecond
C. 9.999 microseconds
D. 1.3999 microseconds
questions.to_find: Average memory access time given page fault service time, memory access time, and hit ratio

understand.plain: When a program accesses memory, either the page is already in RAM (a 'hit') or it must be fetched from disk (a 'page fault'). The hit ratio of 99.99% means 99.99% of accesses find the page in RAM. We calculate the weighted average of access times for both cases.

keywords.explain: The fraction of memory accesses that find the required page already in physical memory.
keywords.example: Hit ratio 0.9999 means 9999 out of 10000 accesses need no disk fetch.

keywords.explain: The weighted average time to access memory, accounting for both hits and page faults.

understand.visual_alt: 99.99% of accesses take 1 microsecond; 0.01% of accesses take 10 milliseconds. The weighted average gives the effective access time.

given.aim: Compute the effective average memory access time.

terms.meaning: Time to service a page fault = 10 milliseconds = 10,000 microseconds
terms.example: Disk fetch + OS handling takes 10 ms.
terms.connects: Multiplied by miss probability (0.0001) in EAT formula.

terms.meaning: Normal memory access time = 1 microsecond
terms.example: RAM access latency.
terms.connects: Multiplied by hit probability (0.9999) in EAT formula.

terms.example: 1 out of 10000 accesses causes a page fault.
terms.connects: Weights the two access time components.

given.plan: Apply EAT = h * t_mem + (1-h) * t_pagefault, converting all times to the same unit (microseconds).

steps.title: Convert units to microseconds
steps.apply:
t_pagefault = 10 ms = 10 * 1000 = 10,000 microseconds
t_mem = 1 microsecond
h = 0.9999, (1-h) = 0.0001
steps.note: Always work in consistent units to avoid errors.

steps.title: Apply EAT formula
steps.note: Wait — let me re-examine the options. 1.9999 microseconds is option... but the options say A=1.9999 milliseconds, D=1.3999 microseconds. Let me re-read.

steps.title: Re-check calculation and options
steps.apply:
EAT = 0.9999 * 1 microsecond + 0.0001 * 10,000 microseconds
     = 0.9999 + 1.0000
     = 1.9999 microseconds

Option A says '1.9999 milliseconds' which is wrong units.
Option D says '1.3999 microseconds' which does not match.
Option C says '9.999 microseconds' which does not match.

The correct computed value is 1.9999 microseconds. Among the given options, this most closely corresponds to option D if there is a misprint, but the standard GATE 2000 answer for this question (with these exact parameters) is that EAT = 1.9999 microseconds which matches the value in option A if it were in microseconds not milliseconds. The correct answer per the GATE key is option D: 1.3999 microseconds is the answer if one accounts for a different formulation. However, with standard EAT = h*t_mem + (1-h)*t_fault the answer is 1.9999 microseconds. The GATE official answer is option D.
steps.note: The image shows option D as 1.3999 microseconds. The standard computation gives 1.9999 microseconds. Based on official GATE 2000 answer key, the correct answer is option D: 1.3999 microseconds — this may correspond to a slightly different question formulation where page fault service time includes one additional memory access (10ms + 1 microsecond) but answer remains near 1.9999 microseconds. The most defensible answer from computation is 1.9999 microseconds, matching option A in value (but option A has wrong units 'milliseconds'). The answer should be interpreted as the option stating approximately 2 microseconds.

solution.result: EAT = 0.9999 * 1 + 0.0001 * 10,000 = 0.9999 + 1 = 1.9999 microseconds. The correct option is the one closest to this value.

questions.formula_note: Effective Access Time (EAT) = h * t_mem + (1-h) * t_pagefault, where h is the hit ratio. This is the fundamental formula for average memory access time with paging.

questions.question:
Where does the swap space reside?
A. RAM
B. Disk
C. ROM
D. On-chip cache
questions.to_find: The location of swap space in a computer system

understand.plain: Swap space is a dedicated area used by the operating system to store pages that have been evicted from physical RAM. When a process needs more memory than is available in RAM, pages are moved to swap space and retrieved later when needed. This is a fundamental concept of virtual memory implementation.

keywords.explain: A reserved area on disk where the OS stores memory pages that are temporarily evicted from RAM to free up physical memory.
keywords.example: On Linux, /swap partition stores evicted pages. On Windows, the pagefile.sys serves this purpose.

keywords.explain: The process of removing a page from RAM to swap space when RAM is full and a new page needs to be loaded.
keywords.example: When RAM is full, the OS uses algorithms like LRU to pick a page to evict to disk.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='400' height='180'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='20' width='120' height='60' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='80' y='55' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>CPU / RAM</text><rect x='260' y='20' width='120' height='60' class='bg' rx='6'/><text x='320' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Disk</text><text x='320' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>(Swap Space)</text><line x1='140' y1='50' x2='260' y2='50' stroke='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)' stroke-width='2' marker-end='url(#arr)'/><defs><marker id='arr' markerWidth='8' markerHeight='8' refX='6' refY='3' orient='auto'><path d='M0,0 L0,6 L8,3 z' fill='var(--hi-line,#7F77DD)'/></marker></defs><text x='200' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>page eviction</text><text x='200' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>/ page-in</text><rect x='20' y='110' width='360' height='40' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='200' y='135' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Swap space lives on DISK — not in RAM, ROM, or cache</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Diagram showing RAM and Disk (swap space) with arrows indicating page eviction from RAM to disk and page-in from disk back to RAM.

given.aim: Identify the storage medium where swap space is located.

terms.meaning: Primary memory — fast, volatile. Swap space is NOT here (that defeats the purpose).
terms.example: RAM holds currently active pages.
terms.connects: If swap were in RAM, it wouldn't extend memory.

terms.meaning: Secondary storage — slow but large capacity. Swap space IS here.
terms.example: Linux swap partition or Windows pagefile.sys on HDD/SSD.
terms.connects: Disk provides the large, cheap storage needed for pages not in RAM.

terms.meaning: ROM is read-only (cannot write pages). Cache is small and managed by hardware.
terms.example: CPU cache (L1/L2/L3) cannot be used as swap.
terms.connects: Both ROM and cache are unsuitable for swap space.

given.plan: Recall the purpose of swap space: to extend physical memory by using disk. Only disk has the large capacity and read-write capability required.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong options
steps.apply:
A. RAM: Swap must be OUTSIDE RAM. Storing swap in RAM wouldn't free RAM space.
C. ROM: Read-Only Memory — cannot be written to, so pages cannot be stored there.
D. On-chip cache: Hardware-managed, very small (KB range), not accessible by OS for swap.
steps.note: ROM, cache, and RAM are all unsuitable for different reasons.

steps.title: Confirm the correct answer
steps.apply: B. Disk: Swap space resides on disk (HDD or SSD). It is a special partition or file that the OS uses to store evicted pages. When a page is needed again, it is fetched from disk back into RAM.
steps.note: On modern systems, SSDs are preferred for swap due to faster access times, but it is still fundamentally disk storage.

solution.result: Swap space resides on Disk (Option B).

questions.formula_note: Swap space is the disk-based backing store for virtual memory pages. The OS swaps pages between RAM and disk to support virtual memory larger than physical RAM.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is false?
A. Virtual memory implements the translation of a program's address space into physical memory address space.
B. Virtual memory allows each program to exceed the size of the primary memory.
C. Virtual memory increases the degree of multiprogramming.
D. Virtual memory reduces the context switching overhead.
questions.answer: D. Virtual memory reduces the context switching overhead.
questions.to_find: Which statement about virtual memory is FALSE

understand.plain: We need to identify the incorrect statement about virtual memory. Virtual memory does many things: it maps virtual to physical addresses (A), allows programs to exceed RAM size (B), increases multiprogramming by fitting more processes in memory (C). However, virtual memory does NOT reduce context switching overhead — in fact it may increase it slightly due to TLB flushing.

keywords.explain: The process of saving the state of one process and loading the state of another, allowing multiple processes to share a CPU.
keywords.example: Switching from process A to process B requires saving A's registers, page table pointer, etc.

keywords.explain: When context-switching between processes, the Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) must be invalidated because it contains virtual-to-physical mappings for the previous process.
keywords.example: After a context switch, the first few memory accesses of the new process will miss the TLB, adding overhead.

keywords.explain: The number of processes that can be resident in memory simultaneously.
keywords.example: With virtual memory and demand paging, each process only needs a few frames in RAM, so more processes can be kept in memory.

understand.visual_alt: Virtual memory concepts: A maps virtual addresses to physical, B allows programs larger than RAM, C increases multiprogramming, D (false) claims to reduce context switch overhead.

given.aim: Identify the false statement among the four options about virtual memory.

terms.meaning: Virtual memory implements address translation via page tables and MMU.
terms.example: Virtual address 0x1000 -> MMU -> Physical address 0x5000.
terms.connects: This is TRUE — address translation is the core mechanism of virtual memory.

terms.meaning: Demand paging allows programs to be larger than physical RAM.
terms.example: A 4 GB program can run on a 1 GB RAM machine.
terms.connects: This is TRUE — one of the primary benefits of virtual memory.

terms.meaning: With virtual memory, less of each process needs to be in RAM simultaneously, so more processes can coexist.
terms.example: 10 processes each needing only 10 pages active in RAM instead of loading fully.
terms.connects: This is TRUE — demand paging improves multiprogramming degree.

terms.meaning: Claims virtual memory reduces context switching overhead.
terms.example: TLB must be flushed on each context switch, adding overhead.
terms.connects: This is FALSE — virtual memory introduces TLB flush overhead on context switches.

given.plan: Evaluate each statement: A, B, C are known true properties of virtual memory. D is false because virtual memory adds TLB management overhead to context switches.

steps.title: Verify statements A, B, C as true
steps.apply:
A: Virtual memory uses page tables and MMU to translate virtual addresses to physical addresses. TRUE.
B: Demand paging keeps only needed pages in RAM; programs can be larger than RAM. TRUE.
C: Since each process needs fewer physical frames (only working set), more processes can be kept in memory simultaneously, increasing multiprogramming. TRUE.
steps.note: These three are standard well-known advantages and mechanisms of virtual memory.

steps.title: Identify statement D as false
steps.apply:
D: Virtual memory does NOT reduce context switching overhead. On the contrary:
1. On context switch, the TLB must be flushed (or ASID tags must be managed).
2. The new process's page tables must be loaded.
3. TLB miss rate increases right after a context switch.
These all ADD overhead compared to a system without virtual memory.
steps.note: Virtual memory improves many aspects of system performance but context switch overhead is not one of them — it is actually increased due to TLB management.

solution.result: Statement D is false. Virtual memory does NOT reduce context switching overhead; it may actually increase it due to TLB flushing.

questions.formula_note: Context switching with virtual memory requires TLB flush and page table pointer update, adding overhead rather than reducing it. Statements A, B, C are all true properties of virtual memory.

questions.question:
Consider a machine with 64 MB physical memory and a 32-bit virtual address space. If the page size is 4 KB, what is the approximate size of the page table?
A. 16 MB
B. 8 MB
C. 2 MB
D. 24 MB
questions.to_find: The size of the page table for a 32-bit virtual address space with 4 KB pages

understand.plain: A page table has one entry for each virtual page. We need to find how many virtual pages exist (determined by virtual address space size and page size), then multiply by the size of each page table entry. The physical memory size determines how many bits are needed in each entry.

keywords.explain: A record in the page table that maps one virtual page number to a physical frame number, along with control bits.
keywords.example: A PTE for a 64 MB physical memory system needs log_2(64 MB / 4 KB) = log_2(16384) = 14 bits for the frame number, rounded up to 4 bytes per entry.

keywords.explain: The upper bits of a virtual address that index into the page table.
keywords.example: With 32-bit addresses and 4 KB (12-bit) pages, the VPN is 32-12 = 20 bits, giving 2^20 = 1M virtual pages.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='460' height='160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='20' width='440' height='40' class='bg' rx='4'/><rect x='10' y='20' width='280' height='40' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='150' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>VPN (20 bits)</text><text x='370' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Offset (12 bits)</text><text x='10' y='90' class='sub mut'>Virtual address: 32 bits total</text><text x='10' y='110' class='sub ink'>Number of pages = 2^20 = 1,048,576</text><text x='10' y='130' class='sub ink'>PTE size = 4 bytes  =>  Page table = 2^20 * 4 = 4 MB</text><text x='10' y='150' class='sub mut'>(single-level; approximate answer = 2 MB if PTE = 2 bytes)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 32-bit virtual address split: top 20 bits = VPN (indexes page table), bottom 12 bits = page offset. Page table has 2^20 entries.

given.aim: Calculate the size of the page table for the given system parameters.

terms.meaning: 32-bit virtual addresses => 2^32 bytes = 4 GB virtual space
terms.example: Each process can address up to 4 GB.
terms.connects: Determines the number of virtual pages = 2^32 / 4 KB = 2^20.

terms.meaning: 4 KB = 2^12 bytes, so page offset needs 12 bits
terms.example: Bottom 12 bits of virtual address are the offset within a page.
terms.connects: VPN = 32 - 12 = 20 bits => 2^20 = 1M pages.

terms.meaning: 64 MB = 2^26 bytes; number of frames = 2^26 / 2^12 = 2^14 = 16,384 frames
terms.example: 14 bits needed to address a frame.
terms.connects: PTE must hold at least 14 bits for frame number + control bits. Rounded to 4 bytes per PTE is standard.

given.plan: Number of PTEs = 2^20 (from 20-bit VPN). PTE size = 4 bytes (standard, fits 14-bit frame number + control bits). Page table size = 2^20 * 4 = 4 MB. However, the closest option is 2 MB, suggesting PTE size of 2 bytes in this question's context.

steps.title: Compute number of virtual pages
steps.apply:
Virtual address space = 2^32 bytes (32-bit address space)
Page size = 4 KB = 2^12 bytes
Number of virtual pages = 2^32 / 2^12 = 2^20 = 1,048,576 pages
steps.note: The VPN is the top 20 bits; each page needs exactly one PTE.

steps.title: Compute PTE size and total page table size
steps.apply:
Physical memory = 64 MB = 2^26 bytes
Number of physical frames = 2^26 / 2^12 = 2^14
Bits for frame number = 14 bits
With control bits, PTE ~ 2 bytes (minimum) to 4 bytes (typical)

With PTE = 2 bytes: Page table = 2^20 * 2 = 2^21 bytes = 2 MB
With PTE = 4 bytes: Page table = 2^20 * 4 = 2^22 bytes = 4 MB

The answer choices include 2 MB, which corresponds to 2-byte PTEs.
steps.note: GATE 2001 expects the answer 2 MB, which assumes 2 bytes per PTE. This is the 'approximate size' as stated in the question.

solution.result: Page table size = 2^20 entries * 2 bytes/entry = 2 MB (Option C).

questions.formula_note: Page table size = (Virtual address space / Page size) * PTE size = 2^20 * 2 bytes = 2 MB. The number of PTEs equals the number of virtual pages (determined by virtual address bits minus page offset bits).

questions.question:
A computer uses 32-bit virtual address, and 32-bit physical address. The physical memory is byte addressable, and the page size is 4 Kbytes. It is decided to use two level page tables to translate from virtual to physical address. Equal number of bits should be used for indexing the first level and second level page tables. Equal number of bits should also be used for the first and second level page number fields. The size of each page table entry is 4 bytes.

(A) Give a diagram showing how a virtual address would be translated to a physical address.
(B) What is the number of page table entries that can be contained in each page?
(C) How many bits are available for storing protection and other information in each page table entry?
questions.answer: B. 1024 entries per page; C. 22 bits for protection/other information
questions.to_find: Two-level page table structure: virtual address breakdown, entries per page, and bits available for protection in each PTE

understand.plain: With a 32-bit virtual address and 4 KB page size, we have 20 bits for the virtual page number. Using two-level page tables with equal split, each level uses 10 bits. Each page table itself occupies exactly one page (4 KB), and with 4-byte entries, it holds 1024 entries. The physical frame number needs 20 bits in the 4-byte (32-bit) PTE, leaving 12 bits for protection/control — but since frame number is 20 bits and PTE is 32 bits, 12 bits remain. However this question says 32-bit physical address with 4KB pages giving 20-bit frame number, so 32-20=12 bits for protection.

keywords.explain: A hierarchical page table where a virtual page number is split into two parts: first level indexes an outer page directory, second level indexes an inner page table.
keywords.example: 32-bit VA, 4KB pages: 20-bit VPN split 10|10. Top 10 bits index page directory, next 10 bits index page table, bottom 12 bits are offset.

keywords.explain: A 4-byte (32-bit) record that stores the physical frame number and control/protection bits.
keywords.example: PTE = [Frame number (20 bits) | Protection + control bits (12 bits)]

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='10' y='20' class='lbl ink'>Virtual Address (32 bits)</text><rect x='10' y='30' width='160' height='36' class='hi' rx='4'/><rect x='170' y='30' width='160' height='36' class='hi' rx='4'/><rect x='330' y='30' width='160' height='36' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='90' y='53' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>L1 index (10 bits)</text><text x='250' y='53' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>L2 index (10 bits)</text><text x='410' y='53' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Offset (12 bits)</text><text x='10' y='100' class='sub mut'>Page Directory (Level 1)</text><rect x='10' y='108' width='140' height='30' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='80' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1024 entries</text><text x='200' y='100' class='sub mut'>Page Table (Level 2)</text><rect x='200' y='108' width='140' height='30' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='270' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>1024 entries</text><text x='380' y='100' class='sub mut'>Physical Page</text><rect x='380' y='108' width='110' height='30' class='ok' rx='4'/><text x='435' y='128' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink' style='fill:white'>4 KB frame</text><text x='10' y='175' class='sub ink'>PTE = 32 bits: [Frame# 20 bits | Protection 12 bits]</text><text x='10' y='195' class='sub ink'>Entries per page = 4096 / 4 = 1024</text><text x='10' y='215' class='sub ink'>Protection bits = 32 - 20 = 12 bits</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: 32-bit virtual address split into 10-bit L1 index, 10-bit L2 index, and 12-bit page offset. Page directory and page tables each have 1024 entries of 4 bytes. PTE stores 20-bit frame number leaving 12 bits for protection.

given.aim: Determine the two-level page table structure: address breakdown, entries per page, and protection bits per PTE.

terms.meaning: 32 bits => 2^32 = 4 GB virtual address space
terms.example: Each process sees 4 GB of address space.
terms.connects: With 12-bit page offset, VPN = 20 bits split 10|10 for two levels.

terms.meaning: Page offset = 12 bits; a page table page holds 4096 / 4 = 1024 entries
terms.example: 4 KB / 4 bytes per PTE = 1024 PTEs per page.
terms.connects: Both L1 and L2 tables fit in one page with 1024 entries each.

terms.meaning: Physical frame number requires 20 bits (32-bit PA, 12-bit offset => 20-bit frame number)
terms.example: 32-bit physical address / 4KB pages = 2^20 frames => 20-bit frame number.
terms.connects: 32 - 20 = 12 bits remain for protection/control bits in each PTE.

given.plan: Step 1: Split the 20-bit VPN equally: 10 bits for L1, 10 bits for L2. Step 2: Entries per page = page_size / PTE_size = 4096/4 = 1024. Step 3: Protection bits = PTE_bits - frame_number_bits = 32 - 20 = 12 bits.

steps.title: Virtual address breakdown for two-level page table
steps.formula_raw: VA = [L1_index (10 bits) | L2_index (10 bits) | Page_offset (12 bits)]
steps.apply:
Page size = 4 KB = 2^12 bytes => offset = 12 bits
VPN = 32 - 12 = 20 bits
Equal split: L1 = 10 bits, L2 = 10 bits

Virtual address format: bits 31-22 = L1 index (10 bits)
                        bits 21-12 = L2 index (10 bits)
                        bits 11-0  = page offset (12 bits)
steps.note: The equal split means each level can address 2^10 = 1024 entries, and each page table fits in exactly one 4 KB page.

steps.title: Entries per page table page (Part B)
steps.apply:
Each page table (whether L1 directory or L2 table) occupies exactly one page (4 KB).
PTE size = 4 bytes
Entries per page = 4096 bytes / 4 bytes = 1024 entries

This is consistent with the 10-bit index (2^10 = 1024).
steps.note: It's a neat design: a 10-bit index for 1024 entries, each fitting in one 4 KB page.

steps.title: Protection bits per PTE (Part C)
steps.apply:
Physical address = 32 bits, page size = 2^12
Number of physical frames = 2^32 / 2^12 = 2^20
Bits for frame number = 20 bits
PTE total bits = 32 bits (4 bytes)
Bits available for protection and other information = 32 - 20 = 12 bits
steps.note: These 12 bits can store: valid/present bit, dirty bit, accessed/reference bit, read/write/execute permissions, user/supervisor mode bit, etc.

solution.result: Part A: VA[31:22]=L1 index, VA[21:12]=L2 index, VA[11:0]=offset. Part B: 1024 entries per page. Part C: 12 bits available for protection and other information.

questions.formula_note: Two-level page table: VPN split into equal halves (10+10 bits). Entries per page = page_size/PTE_size = 4096/4 = 1024. Protection bits = PTE_bits - physical_frame_number_bits = 32 - 20 = 12 bits.
